section.title: The Sins of Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: This chapter presents Jerusalem as 'the city of bloodshed,' cataloguing an almost exhaustive list of sins, violence by princes, oppression of the vulnerable, sexual abominations, bribery, and forgetting God entirely, before turning to two vivid images of coming judgment: Israel as dross to be melted in a smelting furnace, and the sobering statement that God searched for just one person to stand in the gap and found no one.
section.thesis: Jerusalem's corruption runs through every level of society, princes, priests, officials, prophets, and people alike, so completely that God's own search for a single faithful intercessor to stand in the gap comes up empty, exposing humanity's total inability to supply its own mediator and driving the reader to Christ, the one true Mediator who stands in the gap perfectly where every human candidate failed.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Jerusalem, a city of bloodshed, brings her own doom; a catalogue of her sins, violence, oppression, and forgetting God; judgment announced as scattering among the nations to purge uncleanness; Israel pictured as dross to be melted in a smelting furnace; and a final survey of corruption in every class, closing with God's search for someone to stand in the gap, and finding no one.

units.label: A city of bloodshed brings her own doom
units.note: guilt from bloodshed and idolatry alike

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“As for you, son of man, will you judge her? Will you pass judgment on the city of bloodshed? Then confront her with all her abominations
and tell her that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘O city who brings her own doom by shedding {{dam|blood}} within her walls and making idols to defile herself,

you are guilty of the blood you have shed, and you are defiled by the idols you have made. You have brought your days to a close and have come to the end of your years. Therefore I have made you a reproach to the nations and a mockery to all the lands.
Those near and far will mock you, O infamous city, full of turmoil.

units.label: A catalogue of Jerusalem's sins
units.note: violence, idolatry, oppression, and forgetting God

verses.text: See how every prince of Israel within you has used his power to shed blood.
Father and mother are treated with contempt. Within your walls the foreign resident is exploited, the fatherless and the widow are oppressed.
You have despised My holy things and {{chalal|profaned}} My Sabbaths.
Among you are slanderous men bent on bloodshed; within you are those who eat on the mountain shrines and commit acts of indecency.
In you they have uncovered the nakedness of their fathers; in you they violate women during their menstrual impurity.
One man commits an abomination with his neighbor's wife; another wickedly defiles his daughter-in-law; and yet another violates his sister, his own father's daughter.
In you they take bribes to shed blood. You engage in usury, take excess interest, and extort your neighbors. But Me you have forgotten, declares the Lord GOD.
Now look, I strike My hands together against your unjust gain and against the blood you have shed in your midst.
Will your courage endure or your hands be strong in the day I deal with you? I, the LORD, have spoken, and I will act.
I will disperse you among the nations and scatter you throughout the lands; I will purge your uncleanness.
And when you have defiled yourself in the eyes of the nations, then you will know that I am the LORD.’”

units.label: Israel becomes dross in the furnace

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, the house of Israel has become {{sig|dross}} to Me. All of them are copper, tin, iron, and lead inside the furnace; they are but the dross of silver.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Because all of you have become dross, behold, I will gather you into Jerusalem.
Just as one gathers silver, copper, iron, lead, and tin into the furnace to melt with a fiery blast, so I will gather you in My anger and wrath, leave you there, and melt you.
Yes, I will gather you together and blow on you with the fire of My wrath, and you will be melted within the city.
As silver is melted in a furnace, so you will be melted within the city. Then you will know that I, the LORD, have poured out My wrath upon you.’”

units.label: Every class corrupt; no one to stand in the gap
units.note: princes, priests, officials, prophets, and people alike

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, say to her, ‘In the day of indignation, you are a land that has not been cleansed, upon which no rain has fallen.’
The conspiracy of the princes in her midst is like a roaring lion tearing its prey. They devour the people, seize the treasures and precious things, and multiply the widows within her.

Her priests do violence to My law and profane My holy things. They make no distinction between the holy and the common, and they fail to distinguish between the clean and the unclean. They disregard My Sabbaths, so that I am profaned among them.
Her officials within her are like wolves tearing their prey, shedding blood, and destroying lives for dishonest gain.
Her prophets whitewash these deeds by false visions and lying divinations, saying, ‘This is what the Lord GOD says,’ when the LORD has not spoken.
The people of the land have practiced extortion and committed robbery. They have oppressed the poor and needy and have exploited the foreign resident without justice.
I searched for a man among them to repair the wall and stand in the {{perets|gap}} before Me on behalf of the land, so that I should not destroy it. But I found no one.
So I have poured out My indignation upon them and consumed them with the fire of My fury. I have brought their ways down upon their own heads, declares the Lord GOD.”

dam.gloss: Blood, specifically shed blood, bloodguilt. Jerusalem is repeatedly named 'the city of bloodshed,' verses 2-3, her violence against the vulnerable, treated by princes, officials, and citizens alike as the central charge running through this whole indictment.

chalal.gloss: To profane, defile, or treat as common what is holy. Applied here to the Sabbath, and later to the priests themselves, verse 26, who fail to distinguish holy from common, showing how thoroughly Jerusalem's corruption had erased the very distinctions meant to mark her out as God's people.

sig.gloss: Dross, the impure waste skimmed off in metal refining. Applied to the whole house of Israel, this image reframes the coming exile not merely as punishment but as a smelting process, though here, unusually, no pure metal is promised to remain, only judgment poured out.

perets.gloss: A breach or gap in a wall, used metaphorically of a moral and intercessory breach needing someone to stand in it, deflecting judgment. God searched for such an intercessor and 'found no one,' a stark verdict that anticipates the true Intercessor, Christ, who stands in the gap once for all.

basic.context: This chapter presents Jerusalem as 'the city of bloodshed,' cataloguing an almost exhaustive list of sins, violence by princes, oppression of the vulnerable, sexual abominations, bribery, and forgetting God entirely, before turning to two vivid images of coming judgment: Israel as dross to be melted in a smelting furnace, and the sobering statement that God searched for just one person to stand in the gap and found no one.
basic.hermeneutics: This is an extended prophetic indictment, moving through three linked oracles: a general charge of bloodshed and idolatry, a smelting-furnace image of coming judgment as refinement, and a final class-by-class survey of corruption, princes, priests, officials, prophets, and people, that leaves no one exempt and no intercessor found.
basic.originalLanguages: Jerusalem is 'the city of dam, bloodshed,' whose holy things are chalal, profaned, by prince and priest alike. God pictures the nation as sig, dross, to be melted in judgment's furnace, and closes by searching for someone to stand in the perets, breach or gap, in the wall, an intercessor who might turn back His wrath. He finds no one.
basic.typology: God's search for someone to 'stand in the gap' and finding none anticipates the one perfect Intercessor who does stand in the gap for sinners once and for all, Jesus Christ, who both bears the judgment this chapter describes and intercedes without failure where every human mediator fell short.
basic.christ: Verse 30's devastating search, 'I searched for a man among them to repair the wall and stand in the gap... but I found no one,' exposes humanity's complete inability to provide its own mediator. The New Testament answers this exact need in Christ, the one Mediator between God and man, 1 Timothy 2:5, who stands in the gap fully, both absorbing the judgment this chapter describes and interceding perfectly for His people, Hebrews 7:25.
basic.god: God is the offended holy judge, personally cataloguing Jerusalem's sins against the vulnerable, against His own holy things, and against Himself, 'Me you have forgotten'; yet He is also the one actively searching for a rescuer to spare the city, showing that judgment here is not eager but reluctant, sought against only after every possibility of mercy through human intercession has been exhausted.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 1:21-26 similarly pictures Jerusalem's dross needing to be refined by judgment. Ezekiel 13:5 already faults false prophets for failing to 'stand in the gap' before this final indictment. 1 Timothy 2:5 names Christ as the one Mediator between God and man. Hebrews 7:25 describes Christ as always living to intercede for His people, the perfect answer to verse 30's failed search.
basic.oneStory: This is the moment God's search for a single faithful intercessor in Jerusalem comes up completely empty, exposing the total moral collapse of every class of leadership and the desperate need for a mediator no sinner could supply. That need finds its full and final answer in Jesus Christ, the one who stands in the gap perfectly, absorbing judgment and interceding without fail for all who trust Him.

context.literary: Three linked prophetic oracles unified by the refrain 'the word of the LORD came to me,' verses 1, 17, and 23, moving from a general indictment of bloodshed and idolatry, through a smelting-furnace image of coming judgment, to a final, class-by-class survey of corruption among princes, priests, officials, prophets, and people.
context.historical: Addressed to Jerusalem shortly before its final fall, this chapter's catalogue of sins, violent princes, corrupt priests who blur holy and common, false prophets who whitewash sin, closely matches the moral collapse described in Jeremiah's contemporary oracles and 2 Kings' final assessment of Judah's monarchy.
context.canonical: Belongs to the prophetic tradition of comprehensive social indictment found in Isaiah 1 and Micah 3, and its 'stand in the gap' language, also used in Ezekiel 13:5 of failed false prophets, anticipates the New Testament's presentation of Christ as the one true and successful Mediator, 1 Timothy 2:5.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose, in exhaustive and systematic detail, the total moral corruption of every level of Jerusalem's society, and to demonstrate through God's own failed search for an intercessor that the city's judgment, though grievous to God, has become fully and inescapably deserved.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes the specific, comprehensive sins of Jerusalem's various classes and social ills of that particular historical moment; it is not a fixed checklist to mechanically apply elsewhere. Its abiding truth is that corruption among leaders at every level grieves God deeply and that a righteous mediator is desperately, and rightly, sought after wherever sin abounds.

debates.question: Who is in view in verse 30's search for 'a man... to stand in the gap,' and was such an intercessor ever truly available among Jerusalem's people?

views.label: A genuine but unmet historical opportunity for repentance and intercession
views.case: Some read this as God's real, historical search among Jerusalem's actual leaders and prophets, none of whom, in the moment, were willing to intercede faithfully or turn the city from its sin, a tragic but genuine missed opportunity.

views.label: A rhetorical device emphasizing total corruption rather than a literal individual search
views.case: Others read the searching language as a vivid way of stating that not a single person in the entire city met the standard, emphasizing totality rather than describing God literally canvassing individuals one by one.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verdict is the same: no human intercessor was found sufficient in Jerusalem at that moment, a gap only ultimately and perfectly filled by Jesus Christ, the true Mediator who succeeds where every human candidate, in every generation, has failed.

study.originalLanguages: Jerusalem is 'the city of dam, bloodshed,' whose holy things are chalal, profaned, by prince and priest alike. God pictures the nation as sig, dross, to be melted in judgment's furnace, and closes by searching for someone to stand in the perets, breach or gap, in the wall, an intercessor who might turn back His wrath. He finds no one.

typology.type: God's search for someone to 'stand in the gap' before Him, finding no one, Ezekiel 22:30
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the one Mediator between God and man, 1 Timothy 2:5, who stands in the gap perfectly and permanently.

typology.type: Israel pictured as dross melted in a smelting furnace of judgment, Ezekiel 22:17-22
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the refining judgment believers pass through in Christ, whose own once-for-all judgment purifies rather than destroys those who trust Him, 1 Peter 1:6-7.

study.christ: Verse 30's devastating search, 'I searched for a man among them to repair the wall and stand in the gap... but I found no one,' exposes humanity's complete inability to provide its own mediator. The New Testament answers this exact need in Christ, the one Mediator between God and man, 1 Timothy 2:5, who stands in the gap fully, both absorbing the judgment this chapter describes and interceding perfectly and without end for His people, Hebrews 7:25.
study.god: God is the offended holy judge, personally cataloguing Jerusalem's sins against the vulnerable, against His own holy things, and against Himself, 'Me you have forgotten'; yet He is also the one actively searching for a rescuer to spare the city, showing that judgment here is not eager but reluctant, pursued only after every possibility of mercy through a faithful human intercessor has been exhausted.

crossReferences.note: Similarly pictures Jerusalem's dross needing to be refined and purged through coming judgment.
Already faults false prophets for failing to 'stand in the gap' before this chapter's final, devastating indictment.
Names Christ as the one Mediator between God and man, the answer to verse 30's failed search.
Describes Christ as always living to intercede for His people, succeeding where every human candidate failed.

study.oneStory: This is the moment God's search for a single faithful intercessor in Jerusalem comes up completely empty, exposing the total moral collapse of every class of leadership and the desperate need for a mediator no sinner could supply. That need finds its full and final answer in Jesus Christ, the one who stands in the gap perfectly, absorbing judgment and interceding without fail for all who trust Him.

quiz.q: What title does God give Jerusalem at the start of this chapter's indictment?

options.text: The city of bloodshed.
options.note: Verse 2 names Jerusalem directly as 'the city of bloodshed,' the central charge of the whole chapter.

options.text: The city of peace and justice.
options.note: This directly contradicts the chapter's sustained indictment of violence and injustice.

options.text: The city of the temple.
options.note: While the temple is Jerusalem's most sacred site, this chapter's title for the city centers on bloodshed, not its sanctuary.

options.text: The city of exiles.
options.note: Exile is the coming judgment described later in the chapter, not the title given to the city at its opening.

quiz.q: Which classes of people specifically mistreat the vulnerable according to verse 7?

options.text: Only foreign invaders mistreat Jerusalem's own vulnerable citizens.
options.note: Verse 7 describes mistreatment happening within Jerusalem itself, by its own people, not by outside invaders.

options.text: Parents are treated with contempt, and the foreign resident, the fatherless, and the widow are all exploited or oppressed.
options.note: Verse 7 lists exactly these groups as mistreated within Jerusalem's own walls.

options.text: Only the wealthy suffer mistreatment in this account.
options.note: The chapter's concern is specifically for the vulnerable, the fatherless, widows, and foreigners, not the wealthy.

options.text: Only religious leaders are named as victims of mistreatment.
options.note: Verse 7 names parents, foreigners, the fatherless, and widows as victims, not religious leaders specifically.

quiz.q: What image does God use in verses 17-22 to describe coming judgment on Israel?

options.text: A vine transplanted to fertile soil.
options.note: That image belongs to earlier chapters; here the image is metal being smelted in a furnace.

options.text: Israel as dross, melted like metal in a smelting furnace within Jerusalem itself.
options.note: Verses 18-22 describe exactly this image of refining, or smelting, judgment.

options.text: A shepherd gathering scattered sheep back to safety.
options.note: That shepherding image belongs to Ezekiel 34, not this smelting-furnace judgment scene.

options.text: A cedar tree growing taller than all others.
options.note: That cedar imagery belongs to Ezekiel 17 and 31, not this chapter's furnace image.

quiz.q: According to verses 25-29, which groups within Jerusalem are named as corrupt?

options.text: Princes, priests, officials, prophets, and the people of the land, all named in turn.
options.note: Verses 25-29 systematically indict each of these groups by name.

options.text: Only the reigning king is named as corrupt in this passage.
options.note: The passage names a full range of classes, not the king alone, who is not specifically singled out here.

options.text: Only foreign officials stationed in Jerusalem.
options.note: The corrupt groups named are Jerusalem's own princes, priests, officials, prophets, and people, not foreign officials.

options.text: Only the priests and prophets, with officials and princes exempted.
options.note: Princes and officials are also explicitly named as corrupt in verses 25 and 27, not exempted.

quiz.q: What does God say He searched for in verse 30, and what did He find?

options.text: A wealthy patron to fund the temple's repair, and he found several willing donors.
options.note: The search described is for a moral and spiritual intercessor, not a financial patron, and none was found.

options.text: A man to repair the wall and stand in the gap on the land's behalf, and He found no one.
options.note: Verse 30 states this search and its stark outcome directly.

options.text: A new king to replace Zedekiah, and He found a suitable candidate.
options.note: The search is for an intercessor to avert judgment, not a replacement king, and no one was found.

options.text: A prophet to warn the city, and He found several faithful prophets.
options.note: The chapter has just described the prophets themselves as corrupt, verse 28; the search in verse 30 ends in finding no one at all.

questions: Jerusalem is charged with forgetting God even while going through the motions of religious life. Where might your own routine or busyness be quietly crowding out actual remembrance of God?

The catalogue of sins in this chapter includes the powerful exploiting the vulnerable, the fatherless, the widow, the foreign resident. Who are the vulnerable people in your own community that you might be overlooking or, worse, benefiting from at their expense?

God pictures His people as dross needing the fire of judgment to expose what is base and worthless. Is there an area of your life you have avoided examining honestly because you fear what the process might expose?
Every class of leadership in Jerusalem, princes, priests, officials, prophets, is found corrupt in this chapter. Where do you place too much confidence in human leadership rather than in God Himself?

God searched for one person to stand in the gap and found no one, until Christ. How does knowing Jesus is your perfect, permanent intercessor change how you pray for the people and situations you feel most helpless about?

translationNotes.note: 'Uncovered the nakedness of their fathers' echoes the specific language of forbidden sexual relations in Leviticus 18, cataloguing violations of the holiness code rather than introducing new categories of sin.

The list of base metals, copper, tin, iron, and lead, mixed with silver, reflects the ancient metallurgical process of refining silver, in which base metals had to be separated out and discarded as worthless dross, sig.

'Stand in the gap' translates language describing an intercessor who blocks a breach in a city's defensive wall, here used metaphorically of someone whose righteousness or intercession might have blocked God's judgment from breaking through against the land.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 22:1-31, grouped into five thematic units. No em or en dashes were present in the source verses. A targeted web search this session found no sermon from the trusted cluster scoped specifically to Ezekiel 22 alone, so sermon status is none.
